nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes and steep drop-offs, typically at depths of 40–60 m (range 10–110 m)

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on sponges, tunicates, and benthic algae. In aquariums, generally advised to offer a varied diet including Spirulina, marine algae, sponge-based angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and other meaty items. Small portions offered several times daily tend to be better accepted than single large feedings.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by NOAA Photo Library, 1278x2273. Sharp, well-lit whole-body shot clearly showing the black dorsal saddle, contrasting white bar, black eye mask, and silvery scaled body diagnostic of A. griffisi.
Photo by Debra Baker, 1180x969. In-situ reef shot, whole fish in natural horizontal pose with clear diagnostic markings; slightly softer with mild blue-green water cast.

summary: The Griffis Angelfish is a rare large angelfish from deep Central Pacific reef slopes, generally considered one of the safer large angels for reef aquariums though known to occasionally nip at corals and clam mantles. Best suited for experienced keepers with spacious, well-established aquariums.
careNotes:
The Griffis Angelfish is an uncommon to rare species in the aquarium trade, sourced primarily from the Central Pacific — Marshall Islands, Solomon Islands, Micronesia, and surrounding areas. In the wild it inhabits outer reef slopes and steep drop-offs, typically at depths between 40–60 m, and this deep-water origin may contribute to the acclimation difficulty that some keepers report with newly imported specimens.

Once settled, the Griffis Angelfish has been known to become territorial, particularly toward other large angelfish and conspecifics. Keeping one specimen per display tank is generally advised.

This species has been documented as protogynous hermaphroditic, meaning individuals are born female and some transition to male with age. There are no known external features that reliably distinguish males from females.

Juvenile coloration features a predominantly white body with a dark upper stripe; adults develop darker overall coloration with a contrasting white bar.

The Griffis Angelfish is generally considered one of the safer large angelfish options for reef aquariums, though it has been known to nip at large-polyp stony corals, some soft corals, and the mantles of tridacnid clams. Close observation in the weeks following introduction is strongly advised.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source. Initially returned HTTP 503; successfully fetched on retry. Provided size (10 in), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), tank min (100 gal), care level (moderate), temperament (semi-aggressive), reef safe (with caution), diet (omnivore).

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Secondary source. Provided size (12 in / 30.5 cm), tank min (75 gal / 284 L), care level (intermediate), temperament (peaceful), diet (omnivore), reef safe (with caution), native range (Indo-Pacific, Solomon). Rated temperament more conservatively than other sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-verification for taxonomy and size. Confirmed family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes, max size 30 cm TL, temperature 23.4–29°C. Native range: Pacific Ocean, Indo-Malayan region eastward to the Line Islands. Depth: 10–110 m, typically 40–60 m.

additional.name: TropicalFishKeeping
additional.notes: Additional cross-verification. Size 10 in (25.4 cm), tank 125 gal (473 L), temperature 73.4–82.4°F (23.0–28.0°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, care level very difficult/expert, temperament semi-aggressive, reef with caution. Lifespan listed as 5 years (single-source estimate, not included in main field). Detailed native range and diet notes used in careNotes.
